title: Where does Bo Nix rank in 2nd year qbs?
text:
Here is some background for my story.


Code
library(tidyverse)
library(dplyr)

logs <- read.csv("data/2025qbs.csv")




Code
head(logs)

Code
qbs <- logs |>
  filter(
    Player %in% c(
      "Bo Nix",
      "Caleb Williams",
      "Jayden Daniels",
      "Drake Maye",
      "Michael Penix Jr.",
      "J.J. McCarthy",
      "Spencer Rattler"
    ))

ggplot(data = qbs, aes(x = reorder(Player, Yds), y = Yds, fill = Player)) + 
  geom_bar(stat = "identity") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "Jayden Daniels" = "brown",
    "Spencer Rattler" = "black",
    "J.J. McCarthy" = "purple",
    "Bo Nix" = "orange",
    "Caleb Williams" = "navy",
    "Drake Maye" = "red",
    "Michael Penix Jr." = "darkred"
  )) +
  labs(
    title = "2025 Passing Yards for 2nd-Year QBs",
    x = "2nd-Year QBs",
    y = "Total Passing Yards",
    subtitle = "Which 2nd-year QB had the most passing yards?",
    caption = "By: Trey Gibbs | Source: Sports Reference"
  ) +
  theme_classic() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 14),
    axis.text = element_text(size = 10),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1)
  )











Code
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
library(ggrepel)

draft_qbs <- c(
  "Bo Nix",
  "Caleb Williams",
  "Jayden Daniels",
  "Drake Maye",
  "Michael Penix Jr.",
  "J.J. McCarthy",
  "Spencer Rattler"
)

ggplot(data = logs, 
       aes(x = TD, y = Int)) +
  
  geom_point(
    aes(
      size = TD / Int,
      color = Player %in% draft_qbs
    ),
    alpha = 0.7
  ) +
  
  geom_text_repel(
    data = logs |> 
      filter(Player %in% draft_qbs),
    aes(label = Player),
    size = 4
  ) +
  
  scale_color_manual(
    values = c("FALSE" = "gray", "TRUE" = "red")
  ) +
  
  labs(
    title = "Touchdown-to-Interception Ratio for NFL Quarterbacks",
    subtitle = "2024 Draft Class Quarterbacks Highlighted",
    x = "Touchdowns",
    y = "Interceptions",
    size = "TD-INT Ratio",
    caption = "By: Trey Gibbs | Source: Sports Reference"
  ) +
  
  theme_classic() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 14),
    axis.text = element_text(size = 10),
    legend.position = "right"
  )











Code
ggplot(data = qbs, aes(x = reorder(Player, GWD), y = GWD, fill = Player)) + 
  geom_bar(stat = "identity") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "Jayden Daniels" = "brown",
    "Spencer Rattler" = "black",
    "J.J. McCarthy" = "purple",
    "Bo Nix" = "orange",
    "Caleb Williams" = "navy",
    "Drake Maye" = "red",
    "Michael Penix Jr." = "darkred"
  )) +
  labs(
    title = "Who is the most clutch out of the 2024 Draft QBS?",
    x = "Quarterback",
    y = "Game-Winning Drives",
    caption = "By: Trey Gibbs | Source: Sports Reference"
  ) +
  theme_classic() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 14),
    axis.text = element_text(size = 10),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1)
  )

title: About
text: Hello! My name is Trey Gibbs, and I am currently a sophomore at the University of Nebraska–Lincoln. I am pursuing a double major in Sports Media and Broadcasting, and I have a strong passion for storytelling and covering athletics. My long-term goal is to become a sports commentator, where I can combine my knowledge of sports with my communication skills to inform and engage audiences.

title: Does Nebraska Basketball live and die by the 3?
text:
Pinnacle Bank Arena gets loud when the shots start falling. The energy builds, the crowd rises, and suddenly Nebraska basketball looks like a team that can beat anyone in the Big Ten. But when the three-pointers stop dropping, the momentum disappears just as quickly.
For the Huskers, the story of the season has been simple: the three-point shot isn’t just a weapon. It’s the engine that makes the team go.
The question was, just how much does Nebraska’s success depend on shooting from beyond the arc?
Is it just a coincidence when they win, or does their performance from three truly decide the outcome?
News flash: the numbers make it pretty clear.


Code
library(tidyverse)
library(dplyr)

logs <- read.csv("data/nebmensbball.csv")

ggplot(logs, aes(x = X3P., y = Rslt, color = Rslt)) +
  geom_jitter(height = 0.15, size = 3) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("W" = "green", "L" = "red")) +
  labs(
    title = "Effect of Nebraska 3-Point Shooting Percentage on Game Result",
    x = "3-Point Shooting Percentage",
    y = "Game Result",
    subtitle="How does the way Nebraska shoots from 3 effect how many games they win?",
    caption="By: Trey Gibbs  |  Source: Sports Reference: 2025-26 Nebraska Cornhuskers Men's Gamelogs") +
  theme_classic() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 14),
    axis.text = element_text(size = 10),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1)
  )









Looking at the scatter plot of Nebraska’s three-point percentage and game results, a pattern begins to emerge. Wins tend to cluster around stronger shooting performances, while losses appear more frequently when the Huskers struggle from deep.
Basketball is a game of runs, but in Nebraska’s case, it often becomes a game of range. When the team shoots well from three, they put themselves in position to control the tempo, stretch defenses, and create easier scoring opportunities. When the shots aren’t falling, the offense becomes harder to sustain.
The relationship isn’t perfect, but the trend is impossible to ignore.
But what about scoring? Does shooting better from three actually translate into more points on the scoreboard?


Code
avg_pts<-mean(logs$Tm)

avg_3pt<-mean(logs$X3P.)

logs$ThreePtGroup <- ifelse(
  logs$X3P. > avg_3pt,
  "Above Season Average",
  "Below Season Average"
)

avg_points <- logs |>
  group_by(ThreePtGroup) |>
  summarize(AveragePoints = mean(Tm, na.rm = TRUE))

ggplot(avg_points, aes(x = ThreePtGroup, y = AveragePoints, 
                       fill = ThreePtGroup)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Above Season Average" = "green", "Below Season Average" = "red")) +
  labs(
    title = "Nebraska Average Points Scored Based on 3-Point Shooting Performance",
    x = "3-Point Shooting Relative to Season Average",
    y = "Average Points Scored",
    subtitle="How does the way Nebraska shoots from 3 effect how many points they score?",
    caption="By: Trey Gibbs  |  Source: Sports Reference: 2025-26 Nebraska Cornhuskers Men's Gamelogs")+
  theme_classic() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 14),
    axis.text = element_text(size = 10),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1)
  )









The bar chart comparing games above and below Nebraska’s season-average three-point percentage tells the next part of the story. When the Huskers shoot better than their typical rate from beyond the arc, their scoring output rises noticeably. More efficient shooting leads to more points. More points create pressure on opponents. And that pressure often turns into wins.
On the flip side, when Nebraska falls below its average from three, the offense slows down. Possessions become tougher, spacing shrinks, and scoring totals drop. The three-pointer doesn’t just influence whether Nebraska wins or loses. It directly impacts how explosive the offense can be.
Now we have seen how shooting affects results and scoring, but what about consistency over the course of the season? When did the Huskers get hot, and when did they cool off?


Code
ggplot(logs, aes(x = factor(Gtm))) +
  geom_line(aes(y = X3P., group = 1), color = "black", size = 1) +
  geom_point(data = subset(logs, Rslt == "W"),
             aes(y = X3P.), color = "green", size = 5) +
  geom_point(data = subset(logs, Rslt == "L"),
             aes(y = X3P.), color = "red", size = 5) +
  labs(
    title = "Nebraska 3-Point Shooting % and Game Outcomes",
    x = "Game Number",
    y = "3-Point Percentage",
    subtitle="How does the way Nebraska shoots from 3 effect the game result?",
    caption="By: Trey Gibbs  |  Source: Sports Reference: 2025-26 Nebraska Cornhuskers Men's Gamelogs")+
  theme_classic() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 14),
    axis.text = element_text(size = 10),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1)
  )









The game-by-game line chart shows the rhythm of Nebraska’s season. Stretches of strong shooting often line up with winning performances, while dips in three-point percentage coincide with tougher outcomes.
You can see the streaks form visually—runs of green dots during hot shooting nights and red dots when the shots weren’t there. It highlights how momentum in basketball is often tied to confidence, and confidence frequently starts with seeing the ball go through the net.
For Nebraska, those streaks weren’t random. They reflected the team’s identity. The Huskers are at their best when they are aggressive, confident, and efficient from three.
